--- Page 1 ---
1. What is Manufacturing?
2. Materials in Manufacturing
3. Manufacturing Processes
4. Production Systems
5. Manufacturing Economics
6. Recent Developments in Manufacturing
INTRODUCTION AND 
OVERVIEW OF MANUFACTURING

--- Page 2 ---
Manufacturing is Important
Ôāß Making things has been an essential human activity 
since before recorded history
Ôāß Today, the term manufacturing is used for this 
activity
Ôāß Manufacturing is important to the United States and 
most other developed and developing nations
Ôāß Technologically
Ôāß Economically

--- Page 3 ---
Technological Importance
Ôāß Technology - the application of science to provide 
society and its members with those things that are 
needed or desired
Ôāß Technology affects our daily lives, directly and 
indirectly, in many ways
Ôāß Technology provides the products that help our 
society and its members live better

--- Page 4 ---
Technological Importance
Ôāß What do these products have in common? 
Ôāß They are all manufactured
Ôāß They would not be available to our society if they 
could not be manufactured
Ôāß Manufacturing is the essential factor that makes 
technology possible

--- Page 5 ---
Economic Importance
U.S. Economy
Sector: %GDP
Agriculture and natural resources 5
Construction and public utilities 5
Manufacturing 12
Service industries* 78
100
* includes retail, transportation, banking, 
communication, education, and government

--- Page 6 ---
What is Manufacturing?
Ôāß The word manufacture is derived from two Latin 
words manus (hand) and factus (make); the 
combination means ‚Äúmade by hand‚ÄĚ
Ôāß ‚ÄúMade by hand‚ÄĚ described the fabrication methods 
that were used when the English word 
‚Äúmanufacture‚ÄĚ was first coined around 1567 A.D. 
Ôāß Most modern manufacturing operations are 
accomplished by mechanized and automated 
equipment that is supervised by human workers

--- Page 7 ---
Manufacturing - Technological 
Ôāß Application of physical and chemical processes to 
alter the geometry, properties, and/or appearance of 
a starting material to make parts or products

--- Page 8 ---
Manufacturing - Economic
Ôāß Transformation of materials into items of greater value 
by one or more processing and/or assembly operations

--- Page 9 ---
Manufacturing Example: 
Artificial Heart Valve
Left: Heart valve Right: Starting titanium billet

--- Page 10 ---
Manufacturing Industries
Ôāß Industry consists of enterprises and organizations that 
produce or supply goods and services
Ôāß Industries can be classified as:
1. Primary industries - cultivate and exploit natural 
resources, e.g., agriculture, mining
2. Secondary industries - take the outputs of primary 
industries and convert them into consumer and 
capital goods 
3. Tertiary industries - service sector

--- Page 11 ---
Specific Industries in Each 
Category

--- Page 12 ---
Manufacturing Industries -
continued
Ôāß Secondary industries include manufacturing, 
construction, and electric power generation
Ôāß Manufacturing includes several industries whose 
products are not covered here; e.g., apparel, 
beverages, chemicals, and food processing
Ôāß For our purposes, manufacturing means production of 
hardware
Ôāß Nuts and bolts, forgings, cars, airplanes, digital 
computers, plastic parts, and ceramic products

--- Page 13 ---
Manufactured Products
Ôāß Final products divide into two major classes:
1. Consumer goods - products purchased directly by 
consumers
Ôāß Cars, clothes, TVs, tennis rackets
2. Capital goods - those purchased by companies to 
produce goods and/or provide services
Ôāß Aircraft, computers, communication 
equipment, medical apparatus, trucks, 
machine tools, construction equipment

--- Page 14 ---
Production Quantity Q
Ôāß The quantity of products Q made by a factory has an 
important influence on the way its people, facilities, and 
procedures are organized 
Ôāß Annual quantities can be classified into three ranges: 
Production range Annual Quantity Q
Low production 1 to 100 units 
Medium production 100 to 10,000 units
High production 10,000 to millions of units

--- Page 15 ---
Product Variety P
Ôāß Product variety P refers to different product types or 
models produced in the plant
Ôāß Different products have different features 
Ôāß They are intended for different markets
Ôāß Some have more parts than others
Ôāß The number of different product types made each 
year in a factory can be counted 
Ôāß When the number of product types made in the 
factory is high, this indicates high product variety

--- Page 16 ---
P vs Q in Factory Operations

--- Page 17 ---
More About Product Variety
Ôāß Although P is quantitative, it is much less exact than Q 
because details on how much the designs differ is not 
captured simply by the number of different designs
Ôāß Soft product variety - small differences between 
products, e.g., between car models made on the same 
production line, with many common parts 
Ôāß Hard product variety - products differ substantially, e.g., 
between a small car and a large truck, with few 
common parts (if any)

--- Page 18 ---
Manufacturing Capability
Ôāß A manufacturing plant consists of processes and 
systems (and people) to transform a certain limited 
range of materials into products of increased value
Ôāß The three building blocks - materials, processes, and 
systems - are the subject of modern manufacturing
Ôāß Manufacturing capability includes:
1. Technological processing capability
2. Physical product limitations
3. Production capacity

--- Page 19 ---
1. Technological Processing 
Capability
Ôāß The set of available manufacturing processes in the 
plant (or company)
Ôāß Certain manufacturing processes are suited to certain 
materials, so by specializing in certain processes, the 
plant is also specializing in certain materials
Ôāß Includes not only the physical processes, but also the 
expertise of the plant personnel 
Ôāß A machine shop cannot roll steel
Ôāß A steel mill cannot build cars

--- Page 20 ---
2. Physical Product Limitations
Ôāß Given a plant with a certain set of processes, there are 
size and weight limitations on the parts or products that 
can be made in the plant 
Ôāß Product size and weight affect:
Ôāß Production equipment
Ôāß Material handling equipment
Ôāß Production, material handling equipment, and plant 
size must be planned for products that lie within a 
certain size and weight range

--- Page 21 ---
3. Production Capacity
Ôāß Defined as the maximum quantity that a plant can 
produce in a given time period (e.g., month or year) 
under assumed operating conditions
Ôāß Operating conditions refer to number of shifts 
per week, hours per shift, direct labor manning 
levels in the plant, and so on
Ôāß Usually measured in terms of output units, e.g., 
tons of steel or number of cars produced 
Ôāß Also called plant capacity

--- Page 22 ---
Materials in Manufacturing 
Ôāß Most engineering materials can be classified into one of 
three basic categories: 
1. Metals
2. Ceramics
3. Polymers
Ôāß Their chemistries are different, and their mechanical and 
physical properties are different
Ôāß In addition, there is a fourth category:
4. Composites

--- Page 23 ---
Classification of 
engineering 
materials

--- Page 24 ---
1. Metals
Ôāß Usually alloys, which are composed of two or more 
elements, at least one of which is metallic. Two basic 
groups: 
1. Ferrous metals - based on iron, comprises about 
75% of metal tonnage in the world: 
Ôāß Steel and cast iron
2. Nonferrous metals - all other metallic elements 
and their alloys: 
Ôāß Aluminum, copper, nickel, silver, tin, etc.

--- Page 25 ---
2. Ceramics
Ôāß Compounds containing metallic (or semi-metallic) and 
nonmetallic elements. 
Ôāß Typical nonmetallic elements are oxygen, nitrogen, 
and carbon
Ôāß For processing, ceramics divide into:
1. Crystalline ceramics ‚Äď includes traditional 
ceramics, such as clay, and modern ceramics, 
such as alumina (Al2O3)
2. Glasses ‚Äď mostly based on silica (SiO2)

--- Page 26 ---
3. Polymers
Ôāß Compound formed of repeating structural units called 
mers, whose atoms share electrons to form very large 
molecules. Three categories: 
1. Thermoplastic polymers - can be subjected to 
multiple heating and cooling cycles without 
altering molecular structure
2. Thermosetting polymers - molecules chemically 
transform into a rigid structure ‚Äď cannot reheat 
3. Elastomers - shows significant elastic behavior

--- Page 27 ---
4. Composites
Ôāß Material consisting of two or more phases that are 
processed separately and then bonded together to 
achieve properties superior to its constituents
Ôāß Phase - homogeneous material, such as grains of 
identical unit cell structure in a solid metal
Ôāß Usual structure consists of particles or fibers of 
one phase mixed in a second phase 
Ôāß Properties depend on components, physical 
shapes of components, and the way they are 
combined to form the final material

--- Page 28 ---
Manufacturing Processes:
Two Basic Types
1. Processing operations - transform a work material 
from one state of completion to a more advanced 
state
Ôāß Operations that change the geometry, properties, 
or appearance of the starting material
2. Assembly operations - join two or more components to 
create a new entity

--- Page 29 ---
Classification of 
Manufacturing 
Processes

--- Page 30 ---
Processing Operations
Ôāß Alters a material‚Äôs shape, physical properties, or 
appearance in order to add value
Ôāß Three categories of processing operations: 
1. Shaping operations - alter the geometry of the 
starting work material 
2. Property-enhancing operations - improve 
physical properties without changing shape 
3. Surface processing operations - clean, treat, 
coat, or deposit material on surface of work

--- Page 31 ---
Four Categories of 
Shaping Processes
1. Solidification processes - starting material is a heated 
liquid or semifluid
2. Particulate processing - starting material consists of 
powders
3. Deformation processes - starting material is a ductile 
solid (commonly metal)
4. Material removal processes - starting material is a 
ductile or brittle solid 

--- Page 32 ---
Solidification Processes
Ôāß Starting material is heated sufficiently to transform it 
into a liquid or highly plastic state
Ôāß (1) Casting process and (2) casting product

--- Page 33 ---
Solidification Processes
Ôāß Casting:
Ôāß Die casting
Ôāß https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0oibUY8KU
QM
Ôāß https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ThOwfwzes
s8
Ôāß Sand casting
Ôāß https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LmjAQGvSr
F0

--- Page 34 ---
Particulate Processing
Ôāß (1) Starting materials are metal or ceramic powders, 
which are (2) pressed and (3) sintered 

--- Page 35 ---
Particulate Processing
Ôāß Powdered metal process:
Ôāß https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DeM7pRVLqb
k
Ôāß https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=azGg68B-Glk

--- Page 36 ---
Deformation Processes
Ôāß Starting workpart is shaped by application of forces 
that exceed the yield strength of the material
Ôāß Examples: (a) forging and (b) extrusion

--- Page 37 ---
Deformation Processes
Ôāß Forging:
Ôāß https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EKsiT_-swBM
Ôāß https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jF7G0feKMSA
Ôāß https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nBspuKqG9S
w
Ôāß Extrusion:
Ôāß https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vHkwq_2yY9E
Ôāß https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Y75IQksBb0M

--- Page 38 ---
Deformation Processes
Ôāß Sheet metal processes:
Ôāß https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uz1G3z_puug
Ôāß https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Rn31IEOKgQ8

--- Page 39 ---
Material Removal Processes
Ôāß Excess material removed from the starting piece so what 
remains is the desired geometry 
Ôāß Examples: (a) turning, (b) drilling, and (c) milling 

--- Page 40 ---
Material Removal Processes
Ôāß Turning:
Ôāß https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8EsAxOnzEms
Ôāß Drilling
Ôāß Milling:
Ôāß https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dkVs5C1HxSo
Ôāß https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=v2wsZI-jsbo

--- Page 41 ---
Material Removal Processes
Ôāß Gear manufacturing:
Ôāß https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J1UOjBLo-2M
Ôāß https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=K785jfrsKQU
Ôāß https://www.tec-science.com/mechanical-power-
transmission/involute-gear/gear-cutting/
Ôāß Broaching:
Ôāß https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fPkc7PHLlMM

--- Page 42 ---

--- Page 43 ---
Waste in Shaping Processes
Ôāß It is desirable to minimize waste in part shaping
Ôāß Material removal processes are wasteful in the unit 
operations, but molding and particulate processing 
operations waste little material
Ôāß Terminology for minimum waste processes:
Ôāß Net shape processes - little or no waste of the 
starting material and no machining is required 
Ôāß Near net shape processes - when minimum 
machining is required

--- Page 44 ---
Property-Enhancing Processes
Ôāß Processes that improve mechanical or physical 
properties of work material
Ôāß Examples: 
Ôāß Heat treatment of metals and glasses 
Ôāß Sintering of powdered metals and ceramics
Ôāß Part shape is not altered, except unintentionally
Ôāß Example: unintentional warping of a heat treated 
part

--- Page 45 ---
Surface Processing Operations
Ôāß Cleaning - chemical and mechanical processes to 
remove dirt, oil, and other surface contaminants 
Ôāß Surface treatments - mechanical working such as 
sand blasting, and physical processes like diffusion
Ôāß Coating and thin film deposition - coating exterior 
surface of the workpart 
Ôāß Examples:
Ôāß Electroplating
Ôāß Painting

--- Page 46 ---
Assembly Operations
Ôāß Two or more separate parts are joined to form a new 
entity
Ôāß Types of assembly operations:
1. Joining processes ‚Äď create a permanent joint
Ôāß Welding, brazing, soldering, adhesive bonding
Ôāß https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GER8xXvFgNI
2. Mechanical assembly ‚Äď fastening by mechanical methods 
Ôāß Threaded fasteners (screws, bolts and nuts); press 
fitting, expansion fits
Ôāß https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kB6ta-IvFxI

--- Page 47 ---
Production Machines and 
Tooling
Ôāß Manufacturing operations are accomplished using 
machinery and tooling (and people)
Ôāß Types of production machines:
Ôāß Machine tools - power-driven machines used to 
operate cutting tools previously operated manually
Ôāß Other production equipment:
Ôāß Presses
Ôāß Forge hammers,
Ôāß Plastic injection molding machines

--- Page 48 ---
Production Systems
Ôāß People, equipment, and procedures used for the 
materials and processes that constitute a firm's 
manufacturing operations 
Ôāß A manufacturing firm must have systems and 
procedures to efficiently accomplish its production
Ôāß Two categories of production systems: 
Ôāß Production facilities
Ôāß Manufacturing support systems
Ôāß People make the systems work

--- Page 49 ---
Model of the Production System

--- Page 50 ---
Production Facilities
Ôāß The factory, production equipment, and material 
handling systems
Ôāß Includes the plant layout
Ôāß Equipment usually organized into logical groupings, 
called manufacturing systems
Ôāß Examples:
Ôāß Automated production line
Ôāß Machine cell consisting of three machine tools
Ôāß Production facilities "touch" the product

--- Page 51 ---
Facilities vs Product Quantities
Ôāß A company designs its manufacturing systems and 
organizes its factories to serve the particular mission 
of each plant
Ôāß Certain types of production facilities are recognized as 
most appropriate for a given type of manufacturing:
1. Low production ‚Äď 1 to 100
2. Medium production ‚Äď 100 to 10,000
3. High production ‚Äď 10,000 to >1,000,000

--- Page 52 ---
Low Production
Ôāß Job shop is the term used for this type of production 
facility
Ôāß A job shop makes low quantities of specialized and 
customized products
Ôāß Products are typically complex, e.g., space 
capsules, prototype aircraft, special machinery 
Ôāß Equipment in a job shop is general purpose
Ôāß Labor force is highly skilled
Ôāß Designed for maximum flexibility

--- Page 53 ---
Fixed-Position Plant Layout

--- Page 54 ---
Medium Production
Ôāß Two different types of facility, depending on product 
variety:
Ôāß Batch production
Ôāß Suited to medium and hard product variety
Ôāß Setups required between batches
Ôāß Cellular manufacturing
Ôāß Suited to soft product variety
Ôāß Worker cells organized to process parts without 
setups between different part styles

--- Page 55 ---
Process Plant Layout

--- Page 56 ---
Cellular Plant Layout

--- Page 57 ---
High Production
Ôāß Often referred to as mass production
Ôāß High demand for product
Ôāß Manufacturing system dedicated to the 
production of that product 
Ôāß Two categories of mass production: 
1. Quantity production
2. Flow line production

--- Page 58 ---
Quantity Production 
Ôāß Mass production of single parts on single machine or 
small numbers of machines 
Ôāß Typically involves standard machines equipped 
with special tooling
Ôāß Equipment is dedicated full-time to the production 
of one part or product type 
Ôāß Typical layouts used in quantity production are 
process layout and cellular layout

--- Page 59 ---
Flow Line Production
Ôāß Multiple machines or workstations arranged in 
sequence, as in a production line
Ôāß Product is complex - requires multiple processing 
and/or assembly operations
Ôāß Work units are physically moved through the 
sequence to complete the product
Ôāß Workstations and equipment are designed 
specifically for the product to maximize efficiency

--- Page 60 ---
Product Plant Layout

--- Page 61 ---
Manufacturing Support Systems
Ôāß A company must organize itself to design the 
processes and equipment, plan and control 
production, and satisfy product quality requirements 
Ôāß Accomplished by manufacturing support systems
Ôāß The people and procedures by which a 
company manages its production operations 
Ôāß Typical departments: 
Ôāß Manufacturing engineering, Production 
planning and control, Quality control

--- Page 62 ---
Typical Cost Breakdown for a 
Manufactured Product

--- Page 63 ---
Recent Developments 
in Manufacturing
Ôāß Microelectronics
Ôāß Computerization in manufacturing
Ôāß Flexible manufacturing
Ôāß Microfabrication and Nanotechnology
Ôāß Lean production and Six Sigma
Ôāß Globalization and outsourcing
Ôāß Environmentally conscious manufacturing

--- Page 64 ---
Microelectronics
Ôāß Electronic devices that are fabricated on a 
microscopic scale: Integrated circuits (ICs)
Ôāß Today‚Äôs fabrication technologies permit billions of 
components to be included in a single IC
Ôāß A large proportion of the products manufactured 
today are based on microelectronics technology
Ôāß About 2/3 of the products in Table 1.1 are either 
electronics products or their function and 
operation depend on electronics

--- Page 65 ---
Computerization of 
Manufacturing
Ôāß Direct Numerical Control (DNC) was one of the first 
applications of computers in manufacturing (1960s)
Ôāß Mainframe computer remotely controlling multiple 
machine tools
Ôāß Enabled by advances in microelectronics, the cost of 
computers and data processing has been reduced, 
leading to the widespread use of personal computers 
Ôāß To control individual production machines
Ôāß To manage the entire enterprise

--- Page 66 ---
Flexible Manufacturing
Ôāß Although mass production is widely used throughout 
the world, computerization has enabled the 
development of manufacturing systems that can 
cope with product variety
Ôāß Examples:
Ôāß Cellular manufacturing
Ôāß Mixed-model assembly lines
Ôāß Flexible manufacturing systems

--- Page 67 ---
Microfabrication and 
Nanotechnology
Ôāß Microfabrication
Ôāß Processes that make parts and products whose 
feature sizes are in the micron range (10-6 m)
Ôāß Examples: Ink-jet printing heads, compact disks, 
microsensors used in automobiles
Ôāß Nanotechnology
Ôāß Materials and products whose feature sizes are in 
the nanometer range (10-9 m)
Ôāß Examples: Coatings for catalytic converters, flat 
screen TV monitors

--- Page 68 ---
Lean Production and Six Sigma
Ôāß Lean production
Ôāß Doing more work with fewer resources, yet 
achieving higher quality in the final product
Ôāß Underlying objective: elimination of waste in 
manufacturing
Ôāß Six Sigma
Ôāß Quality-focused program that utilizes worker 
teams to accomplish projects aimed at improving 
an organization‚Äôs organizational performance

--- Page 69 ---
Globalization
Ôāß The recognition that we have an international economy 
in which barriers once established by national 
boundaries have been reduced
Ôāß This has enabled the freer flow of goods and 
services, capital, technology, and people among 
regions and countries
Ôāß Once underdeveloped countries such as China, 
India, and Mexico have now developed their 
manufacturing infrastructures and technologies to 
become important producers in the global economy

--- Page 70 ---
Outsourcing
Ôāß Use of outside contractors to perform work that was 
traditionally accomplished in-house
Ôāß Local outsourcing
Ôāß Jobs remain in the U.S.
Ôāß Outsourcing to foreign countries
Ôāß Offshore outsourcing - production in China and 
other overseas locations
Ôāß Near-shore outsourcing - production in Canada, 
Mexico, and Central America

--- Page 71 ---
Environmentally Conscious 
Manufacturing
Ôāß Determining the most efficient use of materials and 
natural resources in production, and minimizing the 
negative consequences on the environment
Ôāß Associated terms: green manufacturing, cleaner 
production, sustainable manufacturing
Ôāß Basic approaches:
1. Design products that minimize environmental 
impact
2. Design processes that are environmentally 
friendly
